title: Polymer clay mushroom figurines
subtitle: A set of three little mushrooms with cap, stem, and painted spots
excerpt: A set of three small polymer clay mushroom sculptures, each built from a separate cap and stem joined before baking. The clay bakes rigid at 110°C in a domestic oven, and the spots are painted on with acrylic after baking. The finished mushrooms sit grouped on a shelf or windowsill as a small still-life set. Decorative only; polymer clay is not food-safe.
sourceNotes: Mushroom sculpting in polymer clay is a contemporary studio motif; the spotted cap visual derives from the long-recognised fly agaric form. Technique derived from first principles. Wikimedia open-source botanical reference for the mushroom-form illustration.

glossaryTerms.definition: PVC-based modelling material that sets in a domestic oven at 110-130°C. Decorative use only; not food-safe even after curing.
The heat process that sets polymer clay in a domestic oven. The clay goes in soft and comes out rigid.

content.text: A small set of three mushrooms makes a charming windowsill or shelf grouping. Each mushroom is built from two pieces of
polymer clay

: a domed cap and a column stem, joined before baking. After baking, white acrylic spots painted onto the red cap finish the classic toadstool look. The clay bakes rigid at 110°C and the finished mushrooms are decorative only. Polymer clay is not food-safe even after curing.

attrs.title: Baking polymer clay
attrs.body: Use a dedicated oven tray. Verify oven temperature with an oven thermometer before baking. Do not share the tray with food cookware.

items.name: Polymer clay, red
items.substitution: Any cap colour: warm orange, terracotta, soft brown

content.text: Conditioning the clay

Squeeze and roll each colour separately in the hands for one or two minutes. Conditioned clay bends in a U without breaking; cold clay crumbles. Keep the two colours on separate surfaces to avoid colour transfer (a single fleck of red into the cream stem reads as a smudge once baked).
Forming the stems

Divide the cream clay into three portions: 18 g, 12 g, and 10 g for a large, medium, and small mushroom. Roll each portion into a smooth column. The large stem is 30 mm tall and 14 mm thick at the base, tapering slightly to 12 mm at the top. The medium stem is 25 mm tall and 12 mm thick. The small stem is 18 mm tall and 10 mm thick. Press each stem flat-base-down on the work surface so it stands upright without tipping; the flat base is what lets the mushroom sit on a shelf later.
Forming the caps

Divide the red clay into the same three portions: 18 g, 12 g, and 10 g. Roll each into a smooth ball. Press each ball flat between the palms to form a dome shape: rounded on top, flat on the bottom. The large cap is 30 mm across and 15 mm tall at the centre; the medium cap is 25 mm across and 12 mm tall; the small cap is 20 mm across and 10 mm tall. The proportion to aim for is a cap roughly as wide as the stem is tall (or a little wider).
Joining cap and stem

Press the flat underside of each cap firmly onto the top of its matching stem. Polymer clay joins cleanly when both surfaces are at the same condition; both freshly conditioned pieces fuse with a firm press, no slip or score needed (unlike pottery clay). Twist the cap a quarter-turn while pressing to seat the join. If the join shows a visible seam, smooth it gently with a finger or with the needle tool tip held flat.
Detail

Press a few shallow vertical lines along the side of each stem with the needle tool, suggesting the stem texture of a real mushroom. Keep the marks subtle; deep marks weaken the column. Smooth the cap surface with a finger to remove any fingerprints. The cap will be painted, so any blemishes on the cap show through; the stem stays unpainted.
Baking

Place the three mushrooms upright on a sheet of greaseproof paper on the baking tray, with at least 3 cm between each. Check the oven temperature with the oven thermometer; aim for 110°C (fan) and verify. Bake for 30 minutes (slightly longer than flat pieces because the stems are thicker).
Curing
sets the clay rigid. Underbaked clay snaps at the cap-to-stem join; overbaked clay browns and goes brittle.
Painting the spots

Let the mushrooms cool on the tray for ten minutes before handling. Using the fine paintbrush and white acrylic, dot small white spots across the top of each cap. Vary the spot sizes: a few larger spots near the centre, smaller spots toward the edge. Five to seven spots on the large cap, three to four on the smaller caps. Let the paint dry for fifteen minutes before adding a second coat for stronger white coverage.
Finishing

Once the paint is fully dry (an hour to be safe), brush a thin coat of acrylic gloss varnish over the cap to seal the paint. A matte varnish reads more natural; a gloss varnish gives the cap a wet-looking sheen. Leave the stem unvarnished for a contrast in finish. Arrange the three mushrooms grouped together on a shelf or windowsill.
Care

Polymer clay is not food-safe. The mushrooms are decorative shelf objects only; do not use as food picks, drink markers, or with anything that touches food. Dust with a soft dry cloth. Keep out of direct sunlight long-term, as some red polymer-clay colours fade over years of strong light.
Variations
Five mushrooms instead of three: scale the clay proportionally and arrange in a small grouping. Mix three reds with two warm orange caps for variety.
Brown chanterelle-style mushrooms: replace the red with a soft brown and the cream with a paler cream; omit the spots. Cap shape can be made more rounded and slightly funnel-shaped at the centre.
Add small white acrylic gills painted on the underside of the cap before final assembly, visible when the mushroom is lifted.

items.symptom: Cap separates from stem when handled after baking
items.cause: Cap and stem at different temperatures when pressed together, so the join did not fuse properly
items.fix: Condition both pieces fresh before you join. Press firmly with a quarter-turn twist. If a piece has come apart, glue it back with two-part epoxy

items.symptom: Mushroom topples and will not stand upright
items.cause: Stem base not flat, or cap too heavy for the stem width
items.fix: Press the stem base flat on the work surface before you join. If the cap is heavy, make the stem base wider on the next mushroom. Or shave the cap thinner with the fettling knife while it is still soft

items.symptom: Spots smudge or smear on the cap
items.cause: Acrylic paint applied too thickly, or second coat applied before first coat dried
items.fix: Use the paint at a normal consistency (thinned with one drop of water if it is very thick); let the first coat dry fifteen minutes before adding a second

items.symptom: Cream stem develops a pink tinge from red colour transfer
items.cause: Same hands or work surface used for both colours; the cap colour transferred onto the stem clay
items.fix: Roll the cream clay first, wash and dry hands, then roll the red. Keep separate boards or paper sheets for each colour
